headline: CATTLE SPLIT RESOLVES THROUGH JOINT WEAKNESS
subheadline: Both live and feeders fall half percent as buyers step back.
lead: The cattle complex resolved this week's question by falling together, not rising. Live cattle closed $241.15, down half a percent, with feeders at $352.88, matching the decline. After three days of convergent strength, both contracts lost momentum as commercial buyers backed away ahead of the long weekend. The split didn't resolve through feeder strength or live weakness. It resolved through joint retreat from buyers who found their levels.
the_takeaway: Cattle buyers found their price ceiling, both contracts retreated together.
teaser: The week's cattle convergence story ended with both sides stepping back from the highs.

one_number.unit: year-over-year decline in Japan's crude oil imports during April
one_number.context: Japan's crude imports cratered 66% year-over-year in April as Middle East supply disruptions forced the world's third-largest oil consumer to scramble for alternatives. When a major importer cuts two-thirds of its crude intake, that's not market noise. That's structural demand destruction that keeps energy input costs soft even as geopolitical premiums unwind.

yesterdays_call.summary: Called geopolitical premium unwinding faster than it built up in crude.
yesterdays_call.note: WTI fell another 0.7% today, extending the Iran-ease selloff into a fifth session.

sections.title: Cattle Buyers Find Ceiling
sections.body: Live cattle closed $241.15, down 0.5%, with feeders matching the decline at $352.88. After Monday's surge and three days of convergent strength, **both contracts hit a buyer ceiling** around current levels. The complex didn't split through feeder strength or live weakness. It split through joint retreat. Commercial buyers who pushed both contracts higher this week found their price tolerance and stepped back. The convergence trade worked, but it worked both ways. When buyers withdraw, even strong technicals don't matter.
sections.catalyst: Commercial cattle buyers stepping back from elevated price levels
sections.bottom_line: Buyers found their ceiling, both contracts retreated in unison.
sections.vs_yesterday: Cattle: convergence narrative held but momentum faded exactly as buyer patience ran out.

sections.title: Grains Mark Time Again
sections.body: Corn closed $4.53, down 3 cents, with beans at $11.98, barely changed. The same range-bound drift that's defined the complex for two weeks. **No catalyst in either direction**, no fund flow worth tracking, no weather premium building despite the eastern Corn Belt staying dry. Dec corn at $4.80 tells you the market isn't worried about yield drag yet. The carry's working, basis is quiet, and the funds are somewhere else. Days like this are how markets build the next move, not make it.
sections.bottom_line: Range-bound drift continues, no catalyst driving direction.

sections.title: Milk Crashes Back to Earth
sections.body: Class III milk crashed 2.2% to $16.38, giving back yesterday's spike and then some. **The dollar strength that hammered milk yesterday reversed**, but the damage held. Milk's sitting 42% up from its 52-week low, which sounds bullish until you realize that's still historically weak for spring flush season. The overnight currency move that drove yesterday's surge unwound faster than it built. Dairy markets are thin, currency-driven moves don't stick without fundamental backing.
sections.catalyst: Dollar strength reversal unwound yesterday's currency-driven milk spike
sections.bottom_line: Yesterday's currency spike unwound, fundamentals still weak.
sections.vs_yesterday: Milk: yesterday's dollar-driven spike reversed as currency move unwound.

outside_the_pit.title: FTC launches formal fertilizer antitrust investigation
outside_the_pit.body: The Federal Trade Commission opened a formal probe into anti-competitive practices in the U.S. fertilizer industry, targeting pricing that's squeezed farmer margins for two years running. When the feds start digging into input cost manipulation, that's not just regulatory theater. That's structural cost relief potentially coming down the pipeline.

outside_the_pit.title: Screwworm detected within 60 miles of US border
outside_the_pit.body: New World screwworm was found in Mexico within 60 miles of the U.S. border, keeping all southern livestock import ports closed. The parasite hasn't been seen in the U.S. since 1966, but proximity this close means biosecurity protocols are about to get expensive for border-state producers.

outside_the_pit.title: Farm bankruptcies hit six-year high in April
outside_the_pit.body: April farm bankruptcies reached a six-year high as the ag economy continues to weaken under margin pressure. When bankruptcy filings spike, that's not a lagging indicator. That's the leading edge of consolidation pressure that reshapes who's left farming when commodity prices finally recover.

spread_to_watch.label: Live cattle / feeder ratio
spread_to_watch.commentary: The ratio held steady even as both contracts fell together, confirming the convergence story played out as a coordinated retreat rather than a relative value shift. When both sides of a spread move in lockstep, the ratio stability tells you the buyers left the entire complex, not just one side of it.

basis.headline: Corn basis steady, crush margins holding gains
basis.body: **Eastern Belt corn basis holding firm** as ethanol demand stays consistent and river logistics run smooth into the long weekend. Soybean processing margins stayed near recent highs despite flat futures, indicating **crush demand is real**, not just paper positioning. When basis holds while futures drift, that's local demand saying something futures aren't hearing.

weekly_thread.question: Will the cattle complex split resolve through feeder strength or live cattle weakness?
weekly_thread.status_text: Neither. The split resolved through joint weakness as commercial buyers found their price ceiling and stepped back from both contracts. The convergence trade worked both ways, up earlier in the week, down together today.

the_more_you_know.title: Range-bound markets: the coiled spring myth
the_more_you_know.body: Corn's traded in a 12-cent range for two weeks, and every trader-blog is calling it a 'coiled spring ready to explode.' But range-bound markets aren't automatically bullish setups. They're information vacuums where price discovery stops because nobody has new conviction. The eastern Corn Belt is dry, but not drought-dry. The funds are positioned, but not heavy. Export demand is steady, but not surging. When every fundamental factor is 'yeah, but,' the range isn't coiling energy. It's waiting for actual news.

watch_list.desc: USDA Crop Progress: corn above 70% planted keeps weather premium minimal
Weekly export sales: soy under 400K MT confirms range-bound fundamentals
Fed cattle trade: reset after long weekend tells if buyers are patient or gone

daily_quote.text: Learn from yesterday, live for today, hope for tomorrow.

source_summary: USDA, CME Group, Energy Information Administration, Federal Trade Commission
